questions matter most?","options":["Structured interview — because consistency of questions makes analysis easier","Semi-structured interview — because it allows following unexpected threads while What is wrong with this question?","options":["It is too long","It is a closed question that can only be answered yes or no","It is a leading question that signals the expected answer and introduces confirmation bias","It uses jargon the participant may not understand"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369256617","text":"Which of the following is the best rewrite of this poor interview question: "Would you use a dark mode if we added one?"","options":[""Do you prefer dark mode or light mode?"",""Have you ever used dark mode on any app?"",""Tell me about how you typically use your phone or laptop in the evenings."",""On a scale of 1–5, how important is dark mode to you?""],"explanation":"Why: The original question asks users to predict their future behaviour — which research shows they are poor at. The correct rewrite instead surfaces the context (evening usage, lighting conditions) that would tell you whether dark mode is actually needed.","correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369286350","text":"You are running a user interview and the participant says: "Yeah, the dashboard is fine I guess." What is the best interviewer response?","options":["Move on to the next question — they have answered","Ask: "Would you say you are satisfied with it?"","Ask: "What would make it better?"","Ask: "Tell me more about what you mean by 'fine.'""],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":3},{"id":"q1774369345350","text":"Why should you avoid hypothetical future-behaviour questions like "Would you pay for this feature?" in user interviews?","options":["They are leading questions that signal the answer","They are double-barrelled questions","Users are poor predictors of their own future behaviour — stated intent rarely matches actual behaviour","They require a much larger sample size to be statistically valid"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369404151","text":"Which of the following represents a correctly structured warm-up question for a user interview about a project management tool?","options":[""Do you like using project management software?"",""How often do you update task statuses?"",""Walk me through what a typical Monday morning looks like for you at work."",""On a scale of 1–10, how productive do you feel using our tool?""],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2}]}},{"weekId":"week-32b3b949","courseId":"course-001","weekNumber":3.4,"title":"Usability Testing ","weekTitle":"","description":"Getting deeper into behavioural understanding of users with UAT","youtubeUrl":"https://youtu.be/BObN8ryKhqs","qaLink":null,"visible":true,"resources":[],"docs":[],"transcript":null,"textContent":null,"assignments":[],"liveRecordedSessions":[],"calendarEvents":[],"createdAt":"2026-03-23T17:03:25.386Z","category":"module","storagePath":null,"storageProvider":null,"url":null,"quiz":{"questions":[{"id":"q1774369439519","text":"A participant in a usability test is struggling to find the "Save" button and says nothing for 45 seconds. The researcher feels the urge to help. What should they do?","options":["Point out the Save button to keep the session moving","Ask: "What would you like to do next?"","Stay silent and observe — the participant's struggle is data about the product's design","End the task and move to the next one"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369477635","text":"Which of the following is a correctly written usability test task?","options":[""Click on the 'New Expense' button and fill in the form."",""Please navigate to the expense reporting section."",""You've just returned from a business trip to Delhi. You spent ₹3,400 on travel. Submit an expense claim for this amount."",""Test the new expense claim feature and tell us what you think.""],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369512786","text":"According to Nielsen's Law, how many participants are typically sufficient to surface approximately 80% of usability issues in a single round of testing?","options":["10–12","20-25","5","15-20"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369540003","text":"A product team wants to test a complex, early-stage prototype of a new onboarding flow where many screens are not yet fully functional. Which testing format is most appropriate and why?","options":["Unmoderated testing — because it scales to more participants quickly","Moderated testing — because the researcher can guide participants through non-functional areas, follow up in real time, and surface reasoning behind behaviour","A/B testing — because it provides statistically significant data","Survey — because usability testing is too expensive at this stage"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369541019","text":"After conducting 5 usability test sessions, a researcher notices that 4 out of 5 participants ignored a prominent blue button and instead scrolled to the bottom of the page to find what they needed. What should the researcher do with this observation?","options":["Note it as a minor issue since 4/5 is not statistically significant","Immediately redesign the button colour","Categorise it as a critical usability issue and include a specific design recommendation — the button is not being perceived as the primary call to action","Run 15 more participants before drawing conclusions"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369594802","text":"A team runs an unmoderated usability test and observes that 70% of users abandon the task at step 4. Which additional research method should they now use, and why?","options":["Run another unmoderated test with a larger sample — more data will clarify the pattern","Conduct a survey asking users to rate step 4 on a satisfaction scale","Conduct moderated interviews or follow-up interviews to understand why users are abandoning at step 4 — the behavioural data shows what but not why"," Redesign step 4 immediately based on the existing data"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":2}]}},{"weekId":"week-4c6ceaae","courseId":"course-001","weekNumber":3.5,"title":"Synthesising your research ","weekTitle":"","description":"Finding value out of the research ","youtubeUrl":"https://youtu.be/2q292g7ExiI","qaLink":null,"visible":true,"resources":[],"docs":[{"label":"Week 3.2 Slides","url":"https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1LqE-MSxiGJ4YI5mmgAaaGX0RGwP4OyMJ?usp=drive_link","id":"doc1774371773045"}],"transcript":null,"textContent":null,"assignments":[],"liveRecordedSessions":[],"calendarEvents":[],"createdAt":"2026-03-23T17:42:13.599Z","category":"module","storagePath":null,"storageProvider":null,"url":null,"quiz":{"questions":[{"id":"q1774369684336","text":"A PM is trying to answer this question: "Why are users from Tier 2 cities churning after their first week, even though they complete onboarding?" Which combination of methods is most appropriate?","options":["A/B test + NPS survey","Analytics review + exit interviews ","Unmoderated usability test + CSAT survey","Focus group + SUS benchmark"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369712054","text":"Place these research methods in the order a well-structured product team would typically run them when exploring a new problem space:","options":["Funnel analytics → Usability testing → Survey → Contextual interviews","Survey → Contextual interviews → Funnel analytics → Usability testing","Contextual interviews → Funnel analytics → Survey → Usability testing ","Usability testing → Contextual interviews → Survey → Funnel analytics"],"explanation":"Why: Interviews first to generate hypotheses. Analytics to quantify patterns. Survey to validate scale. Usability testing to evaluate a proposed solution.","correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1774369712604","text":"A PM says: "We ran 8 user interviews and the finding is clear — let's build the feature." What critical step is missing?","options":["They should have run more interviews first","They should validate the qualitative finding at scale — interviews generate hypotheses; a survey or analytics review is needed to confirm how widespread the issue is before committing to build","They should present the findings to the leadership team before acting","Nothing is missing — 8 interviews is a sufficient sample to build from"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":1},{"id":"q1774369713120","text":"Which of the following best describes the concept of "triangulation" in the context of method selection?","options":["Using three different participant groups in the same study","Running the same study three times to confirm results","Combining qualitative methods (to surface the why) with quantitative methods (to confirm scale) to produce more reliable, defensible research insights","Asking three separate researchers to analyse the same dataset independently"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369713654","text":"A user tells you in an interview: "I always check the notifications tab first thing when I open the app." Your analytics show only 12% of users open the notifications tab within their first session. What does this divergence tell you, and what should you do?","options":["Dismiss the interview finding — data is more reliable than self-report","Trust the interview finding — 12% may be a tracking error","The gap between what this user says they do and what the data shows is a valuable signal. Investigate further — run more interviews and look for context differences (device type, notification settings, user tenure) that explain the gap ","Fix the analytics tracking before drawing any conclusions"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":0},{"id":"q1774369713987","text":" A PM who only ever conducts qualitative research can still build reliable product strategy.","options":["TRUE","FALSE","NOT SURE","MAYBE"],"explanation":null,"correctIndex":1}]}},{"weekId":"week-c09e690a","courseId":"course-001","weekNumber":4.1,"title":"Problem Framing","weekTitle":"","description":"Framing the problem correctly ","youtubeUrl":"https://youtu.be/wGEEkTsMD60","qaLink":null,"visible":true,"resources":[],"docs":[{"id":"doc1775150977634","label":"Week 4 Slides","url":"https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1XBrPzoTzJklRtV341Odb5yKR10S6aMBK/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=110667520258484084197&rtpof=true&sd=true"},{"url":"https://docs.google.com/document/d/1042SxM-Fy-5eBPt3kXNXq4ALtzTa0_yOcXwFW_G2aTg/edit?usp=sharing","label":"Week 4 Resources","id":"doc1775151021151"}],"transcript":null,"textContent":null,"assignments":[],"liveRecordedSessions":[],"calendarEvents":[],"createdAt":"2026-03-31T17:34:00.489Z","category":"module","storagePath":null,"storageProvider":null,"url":null,"quiz":{"questions":[{"id":"q1775150719936","text":"In the 5W+1H framework, which dimension is most likely to reveal the ROOT CAUSE of a problem?","options":["WHO ","WHERE","WHY","WHEN"],"explanation":"WHY is the dimension that probes root cause. The other dimensions describe context; WHY drives to structural causes.","correctIndex":2},{"id":"q1775150757765","text":"Before committing to solve a problem, the two non-negotiable validation questions are:","options":["Is it technically feasible? / Does the CEO approve?"," What value will this create for customer and business? / Why must we solve this NOW?","Do we have engineering capacity? / Is there a competitor doing this?","What is the user persona? / What is the success metric?"],"explanation":"he two questions are: (1) What value does solving this create? (2) Why solve it now?","correctIndex":1},{"id":"q1775150817398","text":"You are a PM. Your CEO arrives with a request to 'add a loyalty programme'. What type of assumption is MOST at risk in this hypothesis?","options":["Feasibility — the email system may not be able to identify dormant users","  Viability — weekly emails may violate GDPR consent rules","Desirability — users may not want progress updates and will unsubscribe","  Ethical — the team hasn't checked if the email copy is accurate"],"explanation":" The scenario explicitly names Monday as the driver — but Monday data is correlation, not causation. Your product team reads these transcripts and concludes that the app itself is fine and the problem is simply user motivation and time management, which is outside the product's control. Which qualitative interpretation trap is the team most likely falling into?","options":["Recency bias: the team is overweighting what these users said most recently and ignoring earlier signals from active users","Small sample extrapolation: 10 interviews is not enough to draw any conclusion about why users churn","Social desirability bias: users are telling you the app is good because they don't want to criticise it to your face, and "I got busy" is a polite deflection that avoids saying what actually went wrong with the experience","Confirmation bias: the team already believes the product is strong, so they are interpreting "I got busy" as validation rather than investigating whether the product failed to create enough value to compete for the user's time"],"explanation":"The pattern across all three quotes is strikingly similar: praise the product, blame themselves. This is the textbook signature of social desirability bias. Users don't want to tell the interviewer (who likely works on the product) that it wasn't good enough. "I got busy" is the most common polite exit line in user research. Option D is close and partially true, but the primary trap here is on the data collection side (users aren't telling you the truth), not the interpretation side (team reading what they want to see). The team can't correct for confirmation bias if the raw data itself is distorted by social desirability.
